comments.title: to help a friend stop smoking
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,
I want to help a friend stop smoking. She wants to stop and has agreed to let me help her.

I plan to pray for her and to make her an amulet. I was thinking of putting in the amulet, lavender to calm her, cinnamon to clear away limits, an amethyst for protection from drugs and some dandelion to overcome negative energies. Then I will open a grove and ask the Gods to bless and strengthen the amulet.

Do you know of an ingredient I should use in the amulet or a practice that works well to help people stop smoking?
Thank you for your attention,
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: to help a friend stop smoking
replies.content: That sounds like a good combination of herbs and stones to me. Go for it.

comments.title: Have you ever heard of these people before?
comments.content:
Hello.

Have you come across a group called the Wayfinders Association of Modern Wizardry in your studies of esoteric groups?

replies.title: Re: Have you ever heard of these people before?
replies.content: Nope. Admittedly I don't deal with the occult community a great deal these days, so they could be all over the place and I might have missed them anyway.

comments.title: Meditation
comments.content:
Hello
I've attempted meditation, Vipassana I guess, for almost 3 years. Initially I was getting good results, I thought. Many positive sensations and easy focus plus lots of long sessions. Then I fell of the wagon and recently wanted to start again. No previous easy access resumed. Images are completely different and I had two very disturbing accounts, one with obscured image but very disturbing feeling to it, and recently such scary creature in clear focus that I am too afraid to go back to meditation.
I only recently discovered your occult teachings; if i attempt to follow your guidance in discursive meditation plus the protective ritual would that in your opinion help against these kind of visions?
Also, what happened to me?

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content:
A lot of people these days are getting this kind of negative reactions from vipassana and other mind-emptying forms of meditation, especially when they're practiced outside the traditional monastic disciplines that give them framing and direction. What occultists call the inner planes -- the mind-side of the cosmos, the part of it we experience through our minds rather than our bodies -- are not all well-behaved sweetness and light! In the present time, in particular, there are a lot of discordant energies and entities out there, and that makes meditation challenging, and even potentially harmful, when it's not combined with other practices.

I'd encourage you, if you find that occultism appeals to you, to learn one of the basic protective rituals (the Sphere of Protection or Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram) first, and practice that every day. When you've done that for a month, then, yes, discursive meditation should be fine. I've never had a student have significant trouble with the kind of experiences you've had, so long as they stuck with regular protective rituals and discursive meditation on occult topics.

One other thing that helps a great deal is prayer. If there's a deity you feel comfortable invoking, call on that deity before you begin to meditate, asking for him or her to protect you and grant you wisdom. I know a lot of people back away from that sort of thing, but the inner planes are inhabited, you know, and there are plenty of beings in them who are wiser and stronger than our species; establishing a good working relationship with such beings is basic common sense.

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content: What is discursive meditatikn?

replies.title: Re: Meditation
replies.content: Meditation that involves focused thinking on a stated theme, filling the mind rather than emptying it. You'll find a detailed description in my book The Druidry Handbook.

comments.title: A Magical Amends
comments.content:
After a bit of deliberation, I've decided to say this publicly, rather than writing privately to you, and I figured Magic Monday was a good time for it.

I first began practicing magic more than five years ago. At the time, I was jobless, living in a slum apartment in a run down city on the California coast. That was, actually, a step up-- two months prior, I'd been living in a tent in someone's back yard in the same town. I was a heavy drinker, unable to hold a job for more than a few months; I was also a dedicated member of the anarchist movement.

This is all by way of setting the stage for the confession, which is as follows:

For the first six months of my magical practice, I stole every single book I used for my practice via the internet, including a number of yours.

The first one was Donald Kraig's Modern Magick. I didn't go looking for it-- I knew that I hated my life, and I needed something to give it meaning. I had been turned on to the idea that magic might be something worth pursuing by The Archdruid Report, which I'd been reading for years. And I remembered that I had had an interest in Wicca in high school, and I wondered if there might be a book on it on The Pirate Bay, a popular torrent site. In fact there was. It was Kraig's book-- which, of course, has nothing to do with Wicca-- and, if I recall correctly, you'd written one of the forwards. I downloaded it and printed out 22 tarot trumps from the internet onto card stock to use with it. And it saved my life. From the very first lesson, I was completely changed. My sleep cycle, which had been roughly 3am to 1pm, normalized (literally). My appetite came back, my cravings for alcohol diminished, and I started to make money-- a very, very small amount of money at first-- but it was more than nothing.

And I immediately began stealing every book on occultism I could find online. As I'm sure you know, there are many of them.

Virtually every person who ever does harm to another does so in the belief that they are right. Because of that, justifications aren't really meaningful. Everyone has a justification. Nevertheless, I'm going to share mine: I was, as I said, an anarchist. I sincerely believed that copyright laws were antiquated relics of the dying capitalist era. I bragged to a friend that, by stealing my book and printing my own tarot cards, I was in fact bringing a new world into being. A post-scarcity anarchist utopia, where 3d printers and similar techno-nonsense guaranteed that no one would need jobs or money. "The future is already here, it just isn't widely distributed yet." All that nonsense.

I worked through the first six lessons of Modern Magick, initiated into that system, and began working through the Celtic Golden Dawn-- for which, after an initial electronic theft, I paid full price at the local book store. This entire process had taken about ten months. By the end of that time, my view on all of this had completely changed. And that isn't really surprising. It would be impossible for me to hold the worldview that I'd learned through my magical practice, and also adhere to the idea that it was morally acceptable for me to steal the work for other magicians without paying for it.

I was, at the time, still very poor. I was doing much better than I had been-- but for "much better" you can read "making around 200 dollars a week, in a good week." But it was enough money to start buying books, and so I did. And I deleted the electronic copies that I had, and swore an oath in my temple to repay every author who had helped me on my journey.

That was quite a while ago, and at this point I have a shelf full of JMG books. But a few weeks ago I realized that there were still a couple of books of yours that I'd read that I hadn't paid for. I had meant to, had been meaning to for a while-- actually for years-- but I'd never gotten around to it, and in the case of two books, had completely forgotten about them.

Tonight I sent in an order to Llewellyn publications for the remaining books that I owe you for.

I would like to take the opportunity to apologize for any hardships that I may have caused you and Sara by depriving you of income from your work. I would also like to apologize for not resolving this sooner.

If there is a question in this, it is-- Is there anything else I can do?

-Steve T

replies.title: Re: A Magical Amends
replies.content: The fact that you went out and bought your own copies of the books, as far as I'm concerned, is ample amends. Thank you.

replies.title: Re: A Magical Amends
replies.content: Dear Steve T, Would you share what helped you normalize your sleep cycle?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Yesterday i managed to perform with my wife and kids the Alban eiler ritual.It went surprisingly well, despite being my first seasonal, not knowing the text entirely, being 2 weeks late, and my 1 year old son burning his hand with the candle ( nothing serious fortunately!)

The questions:
Ceres is a good equivalent of Coel in your opinion?

Where can i find ideas for colective rituals?
One thing i did, is setting a person in each corner who then pick the element and do the circling. But i would like to know how professionals do it ;)

Thanks,

Guillem.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'm not at all sure what's out there in terms of sources for Druid rituals for more than one person. How professionals do it -- well, ask two Druids, get at least three answers! Me, I've been writing my own rituals for more than twenty years, so if I have to convert a ritual to fit some number of people, I simply rewrite it to suit. Remember that you can divide the speeches among different people, and even cut them into pieces, so that person #1 does the first half and person #2 the second half, or what have you.

As for Ceres and Coel, well, do you intend to use Roman deities generally?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ok,then i will try and see what works for me.

As for the gods, Greco-Roman, yes.

comments.title: dreaming
comments.content:
Hello, my question relates to another "waking from dream" experience, part of which, I suspect, takes place in or adjacent to the astral plane. Waking from a dream, but still not completely awake, I find myself holding a plate of food in my hands. There had been an argument concerning this plate in the dream, but its details are slipping away from me. I think to myself: "feed the hungry" and I put down the plate in a place where it can be seen and the food eaten by anyone who is hungry. Up to this, although I have been consciously aware, all has still been in "dreamy" state. Then suddenly, feeling more "awake" I think, hmm, have I just offered to feed ANY auld body? So I add the words "well-intentioned" and repeat myself in "awake" talk "Feed the well-intentioned hungry."

Well, I spent the rest of the day somewhat uneasy. True, you *could* be feeding *anything* in the astral, and I don't particularly want to attract or feed negative energies. Still, the added phrase feels a bit too much like checking whether someone is "deserving" before giving a handout on the street, or whether the local critters are "deserving" when putting my scraps in the compost heap. It would feel more "right" to my "way of being" to let whatever is hungry for a plate of astral food decide for itself that it wants the help and to take it, than for me to stand in judgment. On the other hand, there is this caution I keep hearing, that we should beware of the inhabitants we may find on other planes. I'm really not sure about the implications (either way) of what I did. I would welcome any feedback at all.

replies.title: Re: dreaming
replies.content: Well, to begin with, it was a dream, so its meaning is likely symbolic rather than literal. I'd encourage you to reflect on ways in which you symbolically "feed the hungry," or could do so.

comments.title: God's forms
comments.content:
Dear Archdruid,
Do you think that the anthropomorphization of gods is associated with cultural shifts from a telluric to solar perspective (or societally from unicorn to phoenix to dragon, for that matter)?

That is, there are gods which are conceived in animal form, there are gods that are basically human with animal features (such as the Welsh revival pantheon or later Egyptian gods), and there are gods that are just fully human in appearance (the Greek gods).

Do you think these are on a sliding scale of telluric to balanced to solar, or am I focing a Druidic perspective here?

(Or are gods increasingly anthropomorphized as a society enters ages of increasing abstraction?)

Curious to hear what your take on this is!

Yours in Druidry,
Brigyn

replies.title: Re: God's forms
replies.content: Good question. It doesn't seem to be determined by historical phase -- the gods of Egypt had animal heads straight through from predynastic times to the Roman era -- but I'd expect animal symbolism to reflect more of a telluric focus. Still, that would take careful study of a range of religions.

replies.title: Re: God's forms
replies.content: Guess I have a project then!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You advise against teaching children magic, is that just ritual magic or anything occult at all? If children do learn magic, what sort of problems tend to arise?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That applies to ritual magic specifically. Children can certainly learn basic natural magic (the sort of thing that uses herbs and stones), simple forms of divination, and enough astrology to plant by the moon and make sense of people's sun and moon signs. A basic introduction to occult philosophy will do no harm, either -- knowing about reincarnation, karma, the inner planes, and the like will help pretty much everybody get a more constructive grasp of life.

comments.title: Books
comments.content:
Another question, on an entirely different note:
If one has "The Academy of the Sword", is there a lot of overlap with "The Spirit and the Sword"?

(From my understanding, the one is a living, lodge-based form of Qabalistic self-development, whereas the other is a translation of a manual on a sacred-geometry based form of swordsmanship from the 17th century. That would suggest they contain vastly different material... but do they?)

Yours in the western martial arts,
Brigyn

replies.title: Re: Books
replies.content: They're completely unrelated. The Academy of the Sword is a 17th century manual of rapier fencing based on Pythagorean sacred geometry; The Spirit and the Sword is an adaptation of 19th century military sword technique blended with basic Golden Dawn magic. I don't think they have a single technique in common, since they use very different weapons in very different ways.

replies.title: Re: Books
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: Dean Radin - Real Magic
comments.content:
You may be interested to know that Dean Radin is about to put out a book entitled "Real Magic", where he basically delves, in depth, into the various and voluminous scientific studies that have been undertaken into magical phenomena. Not published yet, but I expect it to be of a high quality.

Potentially of interest is his Youtube talk on it:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SKCyvFZNL7I

He gets most of it right and encapsulates it well for a modern audience (apart from his definition of theurgy), one would almost think he's been reading your blogs.

I do see "Real Magic" as one more marker towards that second religiosity, which is gathering steam.

replies.title: Re: Dean Radin - Real Magic
replies.content: Interesting. I'll keep an eye out for it.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

I have two questions this evening, the first: Are there any dangers in pursuing magic, as a beginner, if you closely follow the instructions in your published works?
And secondly: I was recently reading your essay about "Three Lessons in Operative Magic" and I was wondering if the "power balance" (for lack of a better word) in magic is weighted towards offense or defense? If I can try to clarify, if somebody were to send a working your way that had evil intent would it be generally easy to guard against? Or are the two aspects in balance?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First, if you follow the instructions exactly as given, and you don't have either a serious heart condition or a mental illness serious enough that you're under a psychiatrist's care, magical training has no dangers at all. I've never had a student get into trouble unless they didn't follow directions or had one of those two health issues (and had lied to me about not having them).

Second, it's much easier to defend against hostile magic than to engage in it. A good basic mastery of simple protective skills such as the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, practiced regularly, should keep you safe from anything that other people might happen to throw at you.

comments.title: A question about a place and some denizens in the astral
comments.content:
Dear John Michael Greer,

On your Ecosophia blog Steve T left a comment
https://www.ecosophia.net/the-truths-we-have-in-common/#comment-17228
He writes:
"I imagined pulling a column of light down from the heavens and in my hands forming it into a little green goblin. As forcefully as I could I filled the goblin with the thought, “Remember your shirt,” and then set it next to my bed with instructions to repeat those words when I woke up. I then went to bed and did not think about it again. As soon as I woke up, much earlier than my nature would prefer, I felt, rather than thought, the words “Remember your shirt,” as if they were beamed into my brain. I picked up my shirt, thanked the goblin, and asked it to disperse. After that I started using the goblins, which I referred to in my mind as “memory buggs,” on a regular basis. Mostly I used them to remember things I would otherwise forget, and here their success rate is close to 100%– I don’t know if they’ve ever failed. I also on 2 separate occasions found myself without an alarm clock, and used the buggs to wake up at a certain time. On both occasions they worked flawlessly. This went on, until I decided to get to know the buggs a bit better. I entered into meditation and tried to find out where they came from and what they might want. I discovered a very strange twilit realm of endless, tangled black branches; a colossal tree with no trunk set within a red sky. There appeared to be hundreds, thousands, millions of the things there. I spoke with one, and asked it what I could do for them. It asked for 3 drops of blood, which I gave– in my imagination, that is. I didn’t actually cut myself. Nevertheless, the experience creeped me out enough that I stopped using the buggs. Now, when I need to remember something, I imagine a small angel, rather than a creepy goblin."

I for one would very interested to know if you have anything more to say about that! (What's with the blood?!)

Thanks for taking questions.

Sincerely,

DOPPELGANGER VON LURKING

replies.title: Re: A question about a place and some denizens in the astral
replies.content: You'll have to ask him about that. Astral experiences of that sort are generally quite specific to the person who has them.

replies.title: Re: A question about a place and some denizens in the astral
replies.content:
Hi Doppleganger,

For what it's worth, I tried to find the notes I made about the experience in my magical journey, but I can't seem to find it. What I wrote on the Ecosophia main site is as much as I have to go on, though. The creatures asking for blood may have been entirelyfrom my own psyche-- sometimes, in astral experiences, fears instilled in you by anything from horror movies to Baptist preachers come bubbling to the surface and shift the experience in a darker direction than it would have taken on its own. Or it may be that I was invoking beings of the sort which actually feed on things like blood, and the emotions that come with it-- i.e., fear and pain. I think that this is unlikely, though, because I think that if that were the case, they would have taken a more active and meddlesome role in my physical life. Material blood is better than astral blood, by all accounts. Or it may be some third thing. I don't know-- I decided, like I said, to use more pleasant images when making my mnemonic helpers. I also decided to limit my relationship with them to saying "Thank you for your assistance, now depart unto your habitation in peace and let peace be between us" after the job is done. So far, no one has asked for my blood again.

I don't have anything like JMG's experience with magic and occultism, but I have been doing this regularly for more than 5 years. I have learned, that, unlike in movies and books about magic, in real life you rarely get firm conclusions, resolutions or explanations with these things, especially when you don't pursue them.

-Steve

comments.title: Limits
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,

Many thanks for providing this forum and for your time.

When I was a kid, adults used to tell me that: ‘I could do anything I wanted’. It was clearly an absurd claim, and I realised that at the time because - well, I'm a bit embarrassed to admit it (and I may have mentioned it to you before), but - after watching the dodgy BBC sci-fi show 'The Tomorrow People', and reading Alfred Bester's sci-fi book 'The Stars my destination', I tried really hard to do that teleporting trick. It seemed like a very handy skill to have. But no, it is not possible, and so I clearly couldn't do anything I wanted - and I sure would have like to have done that trick.

You know, I see that story of limitlessness eating peoples lives and energy. And I'm curious whether from an occult point of view, what is the something that is eating their lives and energy? Like is it an entity, or is it a diffusion of energy into the background, or even a diversion of energy away from certain interests? Clearly something is benefiting by that narrative because it comes at a cost to people. Or is it all tied up with where a person exists on the continuum of the great cycle of reincarnation and consciousness? Dunno. I just feel there is more to the story than meets the eye.

Hopefully the answer is not as long as an extended essay! :D

Cheers

Chris

replies.title: Re: Limits
replies.content: Hmm! That's a very good question to which I have no immediate answer. I'll have to mull it over...

replies.title: Re: Limits
replies.content:
Hi Chris, Yes indeed. I too am curious about this, for I do think I completely understand it. I sometimes think of this analogy: a person, say, Fred, who has a very humdrum life, says he's going to see Timbuktu! But Fred doesn't save for his travel expenses. Fred doesn't read about Timbuktu, nor talk to the old lady in his neighborhood who actually lived there for ten years. He never applies for a passport. He does not get his vaccinations. My goodness, he doesn't even get himself a proper suitcase. But, all sparkly eyed, he says he's going to Timbuktu! And if you doubt it, you're a party pooper! My sense is, the dynamic is about enjoying fantasy and getting immediate attention... there is also usually a lot of TV watching in his life... that is, remaining on the couch, motionless as a mummy...

JHG, This is making me think of your discussion of limits.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
(from Myriam – with apologies for the length)

My mother died last week, from refusing to eat. Two weeks prior, a doctor examined her and found nothing wrong. All tests were normal, and she was scheduled for a scope in April for further investigation. She was 81.

But she believed she had something wrong and that the end was near, and because she had a great loathing of doctors and hospitals, she stopped eating, drank very little, and let herself die of starvation. It was quite a moral struggle for those watching to honour her wishes and not interfere, as one usually does when someone is committing suicide. My dad would not have permitted us to interfere anyway, and the rift if we had would have killed him (he is 87 and had a stroke last year).

My mother, the narcissist, controlled everything with an iron fist her whole life, and her death was no different.

It was the most pointless, senseless death I've ever seen. And ugly. She was unrecognizable in the last few days. A simple IV drip would see her right now relatively healthy, alert, and plaguing everyone as normal. But as a good Catholic, and (because of her narcissism,) belief that she was the holy prophet of god, a saint, an incredibly wise old woman everyone should bow to (this is not hyperbole or bitterness, she actually said that of herself) in her mind, she was enduring suffering as a saint, and offered her suffering up so that those in her family who did not accept God's redemption could be redeemed. My dad tried to explain to us some of what was going through her mind, but not all. And she kept insisting no doctor, no hospital. So this is all we have. But in recent conversations with my brother she gave hints to him that she was doing a life review and that she was facing the pain of realizing that her beliefs about herself were not true, and everything she touched failed (my brother's words.) As a narcissist, the pain may have been too much for her to face.

My questions, (your patience and kindness are much appreciated) are along the lines of:
In the Christian understanding, Jesus has redeemed everyone already, but he doesn't force it down anyone's throats. It's our choice to accept it or not. I can't help but feel that my mother is mistaken to think she can redeem anyone with her suffering. She doesn't have the right to decide the direction our souls take. Would you agree with that? Her suffering is for herself only, which I hope works for her. In the occult tradition, each of us must walk our own path in our own way, and no one can interfere with it. Am I right in this? What effects on my own soul, who is currently following a pagan path, would her attempts have?

On her deathbed, she talked about an argument we had three years ago where she thought I had said that I hated her. I told her I never said that and she rolled her eyes, as if exasperated with me for denying it, and I had the feeling that her death wasn't about holy suffering, but extreme control. What I said during the argument, and reiterated on her deathbed, was that she did not have the right to control what I thought, said, did, or felt. Her response was that she forgave me for everything I had done. It was as if asserting that I am my own self was something to be forgiven for. I told her that whatever I had done, it was never my intention to hurt her. That much is true.

She also asked for my forgiveness and when I asked her for what, she didn't answer that. I didn't say anything. It seemed to me her asking me for forgiveness was just something that was supposed to be said, so she said it. It's hard to know how much of all this was because her brain was already shutting down at that point.

The next day, after she died, there was complete pandemonium (some 10 firefighters, paramedics, resuscitators, police (who must attend when someone dies at home) and the coroner) along with all the family members dealing with everyone else questioning what happened, then hours later the undertaker took her body away. I left to go home (my father wanted to be alone) and as I pulled out the driveway, I felt like a hand on my cheek, and felt very strongly a plea for forgiveness. I'm guessing she had her life review and, finally free of a mind that was not functioning right, was able to see clearly the effect she had had on others. JMG, would this occur so soon after death? When we are free of the body that we inhabited, do we immediately have clarity? Or would this come later, after the second death? What exactly is that anyway? Or would the soul have to go through many lives to come to the point where we have developed enough to understand clearly what we have done during our lives?

It is my understanding that forgiveness is for the forgiver, not the forgiven. It is a way for us to let go of any negative feelings we have towards others so that we can be free to move forward. Was she asking me to forgive her so that I would move forward? Or is my forgiveness necessary for her to move on? (If that is the case, my sister is so filled with anger and hatred for her, that she did not come to the funeral (she has refused to see her for decades), and if anyone of us is holding my mother back, it's not me.) More importantly, how clearly does one see after death, and how much of the personality of the person still lingers to colour their thoughts and actions after death? And how would that affect those who remain if she continues to try to have an effect on us?

I am deeply saddened that the incredibly talented, intelligent, creative, artistic person she was could so mess up her life. What she could have done without the narcissism, and what my dad's life could have been without it! In my mind, there is no need to give forgiveness, because she was mentally ill and could not have done otherwise. There is simply nothing to forgive. I simply wish her peace, wish her wellness on her journey, and hope that she has learned what she needed to learn so that she does not have to go through that lesson again. I cannot imagine the pain of having to clearly see and feel the pain and damage she has inflicted on others during her lifetime.

My own journey forward will focus on healing, on healthy boundaries, on notions of the self and the right to assert one's being within a whole, and letting go and acceptance. JMG, your thoughts are much appreciated, and I welcome anything you have to say about all this.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
First of all, I'm very sorry to hear about your loss. That whole sequence of events had to be very difficult for you; please accept my condolences.

For those who are trying to understand the inner side of death, I recommend two books -- Through the Gates of Death by Dion Fortune and A Vision by William Butler Yeats -- in this latter, specifically the chapter titled "The Soul in Judgment." Between them, these two summarize the occult teachings concerning death very well.

The very short form is that neither your compassion nor your sister's anger will have more than a fairly modest influence on the fate of your mother's soul. Who she is will determine what happens to her in the state between lives. Some people achieve quite a bit of clarity immediately after death, if their lack of mental clarity is a function of brain injury or chemistry; others wallow all the more deeply in their delusions. If your mother did achieve clarity -- and that does happen fairly often -- it's a good sign.

In my understanding, for what it's worth, the kind of forgiveness that involves realizing that there's nothing to forgive is the kind that really matters...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you. Your answer is very comforting to me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I am sorry for the passing of your Mother, especially considering the difficulty of how it came to pass; your words were very moving to me, and a couple cathardic tears where shead in Colorado.

It seems to me that forgiveness is a very good thing, especially when something has come to an end. That it is for the benefite of the person doing the forgiving always seemed like an almost half truth; something said to prode a person into forgiving when it is needed. The importance it has for the person who is forgiven, especially if they have asked for forgiveness, has always seemed primare to me.

There are things I desire forgiveness for, or hope that I have been forgiven for, I wouldn't ask because words "I forgive you" wouldn't mean anything to me; but your Mother is beyond the illusionry or words. If there is an explination for my trespasses, and of course there are explinations for every trespass for better or worse, that does anything except dull my desire for forgiveness. If I had acted in a way that I made me desire forgiveness, being told that there was nothing to forgive would be absolutely crushing, even if my tresspassing had followed by direct causality from the axioms of the Cosmos.

If this were a serial, I would be waiting the next issue, hoping the protagonist would forgive her Mother; exactly as I might hope a hero would defeat the villian or pair up with their love interest... maybe this is a niave sentimentality.

I appologize for interjecting here, as it is on the jagged edge of propriety, but felt moved to do so.

Ray Wharton

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ray, I can only speak from my own experiences (and I could be completely wrong), but I feel asking (or wanting) forgiveness from someone is mostly an attempt to mitigate the consequences of our actions for ourselves. If you, for example, tell me that you forgive me (and let go and move on) then I am comforted by the belief that the harm I inflicted on you was less than it actually was, and therefore, the karma that comes my way will be lessened.

The issues I have with this is that, firstly, what happens to me in terms of karma then becomes dependent on what you do (forgive me or not)and I am not comfortable with the thought of handing over the direction of my fate to someone else, and secondly, the fact that I have the chance to evade my karma by imposing my request on you, means that my fears of karma will colour the relationship I have with you by putting some pressure on it.

I said "mostly" in the first sentence. The less usual intent is the attempt to help those whom I have harmed by expressing regret for what I have done, in the hopes that they will let go of any negative emotions towards me, but for their own sake. Not mine.

As for the continuing serial, I'm quite sure I will continue to post on the blog from time to time. You'll probably get the conclusion at some point. :-)

Thank you for your kind words and for being deeply moved. That is a gift in itself.

comments.title: three questions
comments.content:
Dear John,

you have experience in communicating with gods and spirits. If they wish to convey information to you, how do they do it? I assume they don't speak English or any other human language.

This may sound silly to you, but for a long time I've been struggling with my materialist background. It has been a big roadblock on my path to studying magic, so I've been developing intellectual arguments against materialism to cope with it. So far, I think the best argument against materialism is the existence of the laws of nature as something immaterial that rules over matter; and that opens the gates for magic. Do you think its a good argument, are there better ones?

You mentioned that performing magic in Rhode Island felt different, more "crisp" or something to that effect. Is it a special place full of Lovecraftian eldritch beings or is it just a different environment that affects magic?

Diolch yn fawr.

replies.title: Re: three questions
replies.content:
1) When I get an answer to a prayer or the like, it comes in wordless form, as a kind of cognitive feeling -- it's hard to express this in words -- and I can, while that condition persists, feel which words are the closest fit to what I'm perceiving.

2) That's a fine argument, but arguments won't do the trick. Practice magic regularly, and as you start seeing the results pile up, your materialist background will turn into twinkle dust and drift away on the breeze.

3) I have yet to meet a shoggoth or a night-gaunt here in Rhode Island, which is unfortunate -- I'd find them good company. ;-) It's just a different magical environment.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Considering what you've said about made up gods appearing, does that mean things like the Cult of the Supreme Being in the French Revolution and the Godbuilders of the Russian Revolution (both generally regarded with contempt today), could have actually worked?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, if they'd had an adequate grasp of occult philosophy. The Russian school came considerably closer to success -- this may be one of the reasons why the Communist system lasted so much longer than the French Revolutionary one.

comments.title: Choosing a deity?
comments.content:
Hello JMG, I'm a long-time reader and general agnostic about occult matters. While I don't necessarily "believe" in "gods" or whatever, I also don't overlook things which a strictly materialist worldview can't explain. As a result, I'm curious about the effects of choosing a deity, even if in the end they might only function on psychological levels within our own psyche.

Most people seem to treat deities as if they just appeared to a person, or if they were brought up to believe in them and thus accept their existence in a sense without question. For me, I've questioned everything heavily, so thus I don't feel a particular draw towards any deity, theology, or magical school of any sort, and nothing in particular resonates with me either - not that it's a put off, either. There's just no real sense of "oh, clearly Odin is who I should be calling up in my ritual here because he's a positive effect in my life in that space".

What advice would you have for someone in my situation - who can value the possibility of non-material interaction and relationships (even if we don't necessarily believe in all the woo) and want to open our minds to the benefits of a cultivated mental sphere, but don't have any particularly emotional or relational connection to this space and its inhabitants on which to anchor our mindset and give us a frame of reference?

replies.title: Re: Choosing a deity?
replies.content: Start by reading mythology -- preferably the mythology of cultures other than your own (e.g., not the Bible). Make myths and stories about gods and spirits part of your mental furniture. While you're questioning everything, don't forget to question your own assumptions about "the woo." See if any of the mythologies you read appeal to you aesthetically more than others -- whether, say, you like the stories of the Norse deities, or the Greek deities, or whoever, better than the others you read. Now and then, to stretch your mind, imagine that those deities do actually exist in some sense -- try to picture in your mind what the world would be like if they did, and imagine yourself living in a world where that's the case. As you do these things, you'll find your own way to the answers that matter.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I'm wondering if my chart shows any particular strengths I can utilize, or obstacles I'll have to work through, with regards to my practice of the Celtic Golden Dawn system. Obviously I'm not asking for a full reading of the chart, but does anything jump out as far as magical assets and liabilities are concerned? This stuff is Greek to me. In general, which planets/signs favor the practicing mage?

https://photos.app.goo.gl/xezEgG2UKLxRs1an2

Thanks!
Kyle

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The practicing mage takes whatever planetary positions he or she has, and makes the most of them. That's one of the reasons why different people specialize in different kinds of magic. I'd encourage you to pick up a basic book on astrology, the kind of thing that has interpretations for each planet in each sign and house, and work through it with your chart; you'll learn much more that way than you will from me. (A good thorough delineation from a professional astrologer is also worth having.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi Kyle,
With Pluto square your sun-moon conjunction, you may have issues around power and control, either controlling others or fearing that they have power over you. Examine your motives for being interested in magic/spirituality very carefully, and leave all that stuff about power and control to one side as you go ahead.
Neptune in the twelfth, or possibly in the first depending on accuracy of birth time, but either way likely conjunct the ascendant - you are suited to work with images, dreams, visions, stillness, and contemplating the magic within every day physical reality (Capricorn earth sign). Sacrificing yourself for something greater might appeal to you, or losing yourself in a blissful state, but in the end your 'self' (whatever that means to you) is your own to keep and work with/on.
Approach things with your 'heart' or 'intuition', not your 'head'.

With my best wishes for you - I hope this helps.
Christine S

comments.title: astrology's occult aspects
comments.content:
Hi John Michael,
Why do you think quintiles and septiles are largely ignored in classical astrology, both ancient and modern? Do you account for them in your delineations? It has puzzled me for many years why these very fundamental, potent numbers, five and seven, are excluded from any consideration. Granted, they are harder to spot than the five most commonly used aspects but that can't be the reason for their omission. I have two exact (orb of less than one degree -- Moon/Jupiter to Saturn and Venus to Uranus) biquintiles in my natal chart and have observed them to be pretty significant. Many thanks.
Jim W.

replies.title: Re: astrology's occult aspects
replies.content: Remember that the aspects weren't derived from theoretical considerations -- they emerged from empirical studies of the relationship of planetary positions to terrestrial affairs in ancient Mesopotamia. A lot of subtler aspects didn't become clearly noticeable until much more experience, and better planetary tables, made it possible to track them. The quintiles and biquintiles were introduced to astrology by Johannes Kepler, who had the advantage of highly accurate planetary tables produced by Tycho Brahe's decades of close observation; if I recall correctly, the septile came in later. I'm not at all sure why so many current astrologers don't use them -- the classic texts I study most closely, works by Llewellyn George and Ivy Goldstein-Jacobson, use them.

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can do it on its own. You may find, though, that if you do it regularly your interest in other magical practices may pick up...

comments.title: Sacred Geometry
comments.content:
JMG,
I have been meaning to ask this question for several weeks now. One of the things I do for remuneration is mix sound for live events, and as usual for March, I witnessed a lot of Irish dancing, which set me wondering. Do you see strong links between dance and sacred geometry? If so, can you recommend sources on the subject? In the Western tradition- I’m sure the Sufis have a word or two to say about dance…
Berserker

replies.title: Re: Sacred Geometry
replies.content:
I have no idea. It seems reasonable that this should be the case, but it's not something I've looked into, since I have the equivalent of tone-deafness when it comes to most dance.

I'd encourage you, if you want to follow up on this, to take a hard look at Renaissance dance texts -- sacred geometry was much more public in the Renaissance than before or after (thus works like Gerard Thibault's Academy of the Sword, which taught fencing on the basis of sacred geometry, were quite popular then).

comments.title: Weird sensation from astral light
comments.content:
Hello John.

A few years ago, I used to do a meditation which involved, among other things, visualizing a certain symbol at the solar plexus. It gave me some good results, but one minor side-effect was that I started feeling a weird, needle-like sensation on the skin of my back - it was a small, single point at the height of the solar plexus, aligned with the spine. Another way to describe it would be saying it felt like static electricity at a certain place on my back.

I then stopped doing this exercise for various reasons and the weird sensation mostly disappeared, coming back maybe once per month.

Fast forward to today, I've started practicing the Tower of Light exercise from Denning & Phillips' Practical Guide to Psychic Self-Defense. It's been only 3 weeks, but the weird prickling sensation on my skin has returned, exactly in the place where it was previously. Now, it's not unpleasant or anything, but it's definitely a little weird. I feel it at various times during the day.

Do you have any idea of what this might be and what, if anything, I should do about it?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Weird sensation from astral light
replies.content: Okay, you've overstimulated your solar plexus. Try relaxing as much as you can, and breathing energy gently up the midline of your body -- breathing in, bring it up from your feet to the crown of your head; breathing out, bring it back down from your head to your feet. Do that seven times, once each day, and see if it takes care of the sensation.

comments.title: Follow the Yellow Brick Road...TO HELL!!!
comments.content: My community theater group is performing The Wizard of Oz this summer, and I was wanting to find some info about the occult references and correspondences that L. Frank Baum used in the story. The only sources I could find on the internet were either written by extreme fundamentalists who were worried about my soul, or else Illuminati conspiracy-theory types, neither of which are helpful. Are there any sources, online or otherwise, that you can suggest? The only things of which I'm already aware are how Dorothy and her friends each correspond to one of the classic elements.

replies.title: Re: Follow the Yellow Brick Road...TO HELL!!!
replies.content:
L. Frank Baum and his wife, Maud, were actually quite knowledgeable about magic and occultism, especially as they were presented within the magical wing of Spiritualism and within Theosophy. They also had a strong interest in what was then called "psychical research" (scientific investigations of ESP, telekinesis, telepathy, and so forth).

In all these matters, they were strongly influenced by Maud's mother, the famous and redoubtable Matilda Joslyn Gage. She was the most radical of the three early feminists who wrote the multi-volume _History of Woman Suffrage_ (1881). (The other two authors were Elizabeth Cady Stanton and Susan B. Anthony.)

Matilda was also the author of _Woman, Church and State_ (1893), which she wrote while living with the Baums in the same household. In this work she devoted particular attention to witchcraft and witches, arguing that many of the women accused of witchcraft were the best minds of their era, mistresses of the subtle forces of nature which had wholly escaped the attention of men, and also keen critics of the ways in which male-dominated institutions of church and state had always oppressed women.

I don't know of any good scholarship on the Baums' occult and magical interests (nor, for that matter, on Matilda Joslyn Gage's interest in the same things). There are hints in Katharine M. Rogers' biography of L. Frank Baum (2002), but she doesn't seem to have been sufficiently interested to look very deeply into the question.

Robert Mathiesen

replies.title: Re: Follow the Yellow Brick Road...TO HELL!!!
replies.content: A very good question. To my knowledge, though, nobody's done the necessary research.

comments.title: Mental illness and meditation
comments.content:
Hello, and thank you for hosting Magic Monday.

My family has some mental illness. Grandma's sister was hospitalized for a while - I'm not sure the official diagnosis but she had delusions. Grandma, my mother, and my brother share narcissistic tendencies but there's been no official diagnosis. I think I'm in relatively stable mental health, and my husband agrees.

When I meditate, sometimes the experience is intense - insights or ideas flood in, or my power of visualization seems vivid. These intense sessions take energy from me and feel like a mental workout. Sometimes things go awry. Once I "saw" darkness fill my closed-eye vision, and I felt that I was supposed to do a particular action to make things right with a friendship. This was before I started divination, and the "atoning" action was actually interpreted as incredibly hurtful. I've also, throughout my life, had some moving mystical experiences - feelings of connectedness with Creation.

How can I tell if these experiences are part of a natural, healthy-though-novice magical experience or whether they are resulting from underlying mental imbalance that otherwise is not noticeable? And if the latter, what precautions should I take?

replies.title: Re: Mental illness and meditation
replies.content: What you've described is entirely normal, so I wouldn't worry about it. A little narcissism is not going to be a serious issue -- the kind of mental illness that can be problematic for an operative mage is the kind that's serious enough to require a psychiatrist's care.

comments.title: Spells for fortitude & good cheer?
comments.content:
Okay, so I’m picking myself up off the floor after a prolonged and solid drubbing from the fates, trying not to cut hands and knees on the fragments of shattered dreams. I have a plan of action, more or less. It probably will work, if I follow it with due diligence.

But my objective circumstances are not especially promising; assorted swords of Damocles swoop about overhead; my spirits are still pretty much in the dump. In these circs, it’s quite easy for long-established negative tape loops to start playing in one’s head, especially when problems arise (a fairly frequent occurrence), which interferes with the ability to take effective action.

Is there any magic particularly suitable for dealing with this - for keeping oneself on an even keel, psychologically and emotionally? Regular performance of The Rose Cross ritual, perhaps, or something based on it? I think I could manage that.

If I were a Stoic, I suppose I could just remind myself that there are things we can control and those we can’t control, that what can’t be cured must be endured, etc. But in truth I’m not really temperamentally suited to Stoicism; Epicurus always attracted me much more than Epictetus. Can you recommend some other effective approach to maintaining equanimity in the face of difficulties? Some regular practice might provide a sense of continuity, of the ability to cope with even rather severe challenges.

Kevin

replies.title: Re: Spells for fortitude & good cheer?
replies.content:
There's quite a bit of natural magic that might help. The herb borage is noted for its ability to bring fortitude -- "I borage bring courage" is an old rhyme of lore. More generally, herbs and stones corresponding to the sun, Jupiter, and Venus are worth using -- Ficino talks quite a bit about that in his manual on living according to the heavens.

As for philosophy, well, the reason Epicurean philosophy never got that many followers is precisely that it doesn't have much to offer when it comes to hard times. Stoicism does. No, it's not easy, but you might consider working on it anyway.

replies.title: Re: Spells for fortitude & good cheer?
replies.content:
Thanks! I shall seek out borage with due alacrity. And now that you mention it, I do recall that Ficino is very big on the benefics. I shall seek also their symbola and synthemata.

I’ll take Stoicism under advisement. I’m sure you’re right; but when I consider who might be the greatest of philosophers - Plato? Empedocles? Iamblichus? - I find my preference tends to the mind that gave us the phrase “Laissez les bons temps rouler!” A true sage if ever there was one. :)

replies.title: Re: Spells for fortitude & good cheer?
replies.content: Philosophies are like tools -- a good saw is a very poor hammer. Thus, to my mind, there's no one "greatest of philosophers." If the good times are rolling, then Epicurus is your man, but when they aren't -- and it's fairly clear from your comments that for you, right now, they aren't -- you want to pick up Epictetus' Enchiridion and use it to saw your way out from under your present funk.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you think a person can have a non-human soul, but not be aware of it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Probably not.

A few years back there was a fad for claiming a nonhuman soul; all kinds of enthusiastic people proclaimed themselves "otherkin," and insisted that they were "actually" elves, dragons, horses, tyrannosaurs, et cetera ad nauseam. I'm fairly sure there was something interesting going on in a few cases, but it was drowned out by a vast torrent of make-believe and roleplaying, which mostly ended up (of course) in bed, or in fantasies thereof.

Such ideas still pop up fairly often, so it's worth being cautious about such things. If what's going on is simply that you feel baffled and estranged by the human beings around you, well, welcome to the club; that's a common experience many of us have these days.

replies.title: Non-humans
replies.content:
Dion Fortune thought changelings were real, and felt their morals were “of the barnyard” (perhaps they are all in Congress?).

The otherkin thing sounds like more of the “let’s pretend” so popular these days. Maybe the reason so many adults want to pretend is that they find our current reality intolerable.

comments.title: Autism and magic
comments.content:
Last February I started working through your Celtic Golden Dawn book and ended up getting derailed and going on a journey of self discovery instead. I learned a lot, the most stunning discovery was that I am autistic.

It's been rough realizing that they way I experience the world isn't really the same as most people. I spent nearly the last 40 years thinking everyone was working with a similar toolkit and just dismissing those things that didn't line up. There appears to be a notable difference in the role the subconscious plays in autistics and neurotypicals.

My question is is there anything different in the way an autistic should approach magical practice?

replies.title: Re: Autism and magic
replies.content:
Well, I have adult residual Aspergers syndrome, which is of course an autism spectrum disorder, so anything I can do, you can probably do just as well!

More generally, I've noticed that people on the autism spectrum very often do extremely well as occultists. Partly that's our capacity for obsessive focus on special subjects, but I'm not at all sure that's the only thing involved. You'll very likely find that magical training gets results more quickly for you than it does for neurotypical people. Good luck on the Path!

comments.title: Ritual to reinvigorate from burnout
comments.content:
Hi John, Can you recommend a ritual or spell or, better yet, a path for rebuilding one's life energy and enthusiasm and happiness within the traditions you're familiar with? The last few years I've been proceeding mostly on force of will and, sometimes, just anger after a period of previous loss.

I don't have a background in any of the occult areas and don't even know if I believe in that stuff. I've been trying to be more grateful for what I have and also doing some praying but so far it feels like nobody is listening.

Thanks for any suggestions.

replies.title: Re: Ritual to reinvigorate from burnout
replies.content: A spell or a ritual won't do it, and nothing will do it overnight. It sounds as though you've been through a very rough time, and that's going to leave scars that take time to heal. If you can afford it, you might consider seeing a counselor or therapist -- just having the chance to unburden yourself once a week or so to someone knowledgeable and sympathetic could do a lot. Regular massage from a professional will also likely help; so will extravagant amounts of rest and sleep -- the energy body as well as the physical body needs time and rest to recover from trauma. That's what I would recommend for the time being, until you've recovered some of your equilibrium.
